content:
First, note that on the $3\times3$ grid the cells form a bipartite graph: the “even‐sum” cells 
$$A=\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2),(3,1),(3,3)\}$$ 
and the “odd‐sum” cells 
$$B=\{(1,2),(2,1),(2,3),(3,2)\}$$ 
are each independent sets, and every edge goes between $A$ and $B$.  In any arrangement in which every adjacent pair has opposite parity, one whole part must carry all the odds and the other all the evens.  Since there are 5 odd numbers $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$ and only 4 even numbers $\{2,4,6,8\}$, the only possibility is

  – place the 5 odds arbitrarily on the 5 cells of $A$,  
  – place the 4 evens arbitrarily on the 4 cells of $B$.  

Thus without symmetry‐identifications there are 
$$5!\;\times\;4!\;=\;120\cdot24\;=\;2880$$ 
Isth­mian arrangements.

Next we quotient by the action of rotations by $0^\circ,90^\circ,180^\circ,270^\circ$.  By Burnside’s Lemma the number of orbits is
\[
\frac{1}{4}\Bigl(\#\text{fixed by }0^\circ
\;+\;\#\text{fixed by }90^\circ
\;+\;\#\text{fixed by }180^\circ
\;+\;\#\text{fixed by }270^\circ\Bigr).
\]
Clearly “fixed by $0^\circ$” is $2880$.  But a nontrivial rotation permutes cells in cycles of length $2$ or $4$, and since our labels $1,2,\dots,9$ are all distinct no arrangement can be pointwise‐fixed by a nontrivial rotation.  Hence the other three terms all vanish, and we get
\[
\frac{2880+0+0+0}{4}\;=\;720.
\]

Therefore the number of distinct Isthmian arrangements up to rotation is 
\[
\boxed{720}.
\]
